Tip: Welcome to the Investigate a Dataset project! You will find tips in quoted sections like this to help organize your approach to your investigation. Once you complete this project, remove these Tip sections from your report before submission. First things first, you might want to double-click this Markdown cell and change the title so that it reflects your dataset and investigation.

Project: Investigate a Dataset - [Dataset-name]¶

Table of Contents¶

  • Introduction
  • Data Wrangling
  • Exploratory Data Analysis
  • Conclusions

Introduction¶

Dataset Description¶

Tip: In this section of the report, provide a brief introduction to the dataset you've selected/downloaded for analysis. Read through the description available on the Project Details page of Investigate a Dataset lesson for this course. List all column names in each table, and their significance. In case of multiple tables, describe the relationship between tables.

Question(s) for Analysis¶

Question 1: How have revenue and budget changed over the years?¶

Question 2: How does genre affect popularity?¶

Install packages.¶

If some of the code does not work, you may not have the required packages installed. In that case, uncomment the following cell and run it. It is commented out by default to avoid the extra runtime caused by trying to install them.

In [183]:
# Import the required packages for this notebook.
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
In [184]:
# Import the dataset to be used and show the first few rows.
df = pd.read_csv('tmdb-movies.csv')
df.head()

It seems there are a lot of films with values of 0 for budget and revenue, as well as at least one with 0 for runtime. In fact, it seems that at least 50% of all films in the dataset have a budget or revenue of 0.

5 rows × 21 columns

It seems that a lot of the "zero" entries are just incomplete data. This will probably affect analysis in a negative way, so it will likely be necessary to get rid of them. If it's only a small amount, I could probably just add the data manually.

In [187]:
print((df['runtime'] == 0).sum())
print((df['revenue'] == 0).sum())
print((df['budget'] == 0).sum())

31
6016
5696

That is not a small amount. As such, it is probably best to just remove the entries with the incomplete revenue, budget, and runtime. This does mean that we will need to get rid of at least 6000 entries, however. For analysis of revenue and budget, these entries will cause issues. However, for the analysis of genre and popularity, these entries may still have adequate data. As such, I will clean the dataset first, then create a separate dataset without the zero revenue or budget entries.

Data Wrangling¶

Tip: In this section of the report, you will load in the data, check for cleanliness, and then trim and clean your dataset for analysis. Make sure that you document your data cleaning steps in mark-down cells precisely and justify your cleaning decisions.

General Properties¶

Tip: You should not perform too many operations in each cell. Create cells freely to explore your data. One option that you can take with this project is to do a lot of explorations initially. This does not have to be organized, but make sure you use enough comments to understand the purpose of each code cell. Then, after you're done with your analysis, trim the excess and organize your steps so that you have a flowing, cohesive report.

In [190]:
# release_date represents a date, but is listed as an object dtype. Check the actual data type being used in the column.
print(type(df['release_date'][0]))

<class 'str'>

Pandas loads numeric data types as numeric64 by default. However, this causes the dataframe to use far more memory than necessary. The dataset also contains many null values in the non-numeric columns. If they were in numeric columns, various methods could be used to replace the null values with real values, such as by using values of central tendency (mean, median, mode). Because they aren't in numeric columns, they will likely just need to be removed. This will also be done in the data cleaning section.

Before we start cleaning, I want to prepare a way to reduce the size of the dataset to reduce memory usage. The first thing to do is check the maximum numeric values to later change those columns to smaller numeric types.

In [191]:
# Check maximum values in dataset
df.max(numeric_only=True)
Out[191]:
id              4.178590e+05
popularity      3.298576e+01
budget          4.250000e+08
revenue         2.781506e+09
runtime         9.000000e+02
vote_count      9.767000e+03
vote_average    9.200000e+00
release_year    2.015000e+03
budget_adj      4.250000e+08
revenue_adj     2.827124e+09
dtype: float64
In [192]:
# Takes a dataframe and creates a mask dict to change numeric columns to their lowest possible numeric type (i.e. int64 may become int32, int16, or 
# int8). Pandas has a .numeric() method, but the "downcast" argument seems to only allow for either int or float, so different lists would have to 
# be created for both dtypes, then the method would have be called on both lists. Basically, the creation of a new function and using the pandas method 
# would both create fairly complex code, and this code was already created before I learned about the method, so this is the one I chose.

# One 
def lowest_num(dataset):
    """
    Accepts pandas dataframe as singular argument. 
    Prints python dict containing the dataset's numeric columns along with the smallest numeric datatype that the column allows for.
    Returns mask dictionary for typing pandas dataframe with .astype() method

    The way the function creates the mask is by first finding the maximum value in each numeric column. Then, it finds the base2 log of each maximum, 
    finds the next highest power of two, and adds the name of the column and that power of two as a key-value pair to the mask dict.
    """
    # Create list of max values in each column and list of numeric columns.
    max_values = dataset.max(numeric_only=True).to_list()
    max_mask = {}
    num_cols = dataset.select_dtypes(include='number').columns.tolist()
    # ind is used for indexing each list, to avoid the issue of to columns having the same max value. The for loop could also loop through the list 
    # of columns, but I thought of this solution first.
    ind = 0

    # Loop through all of the max values. For each value, find the base2 log; find the next highest power of two; add column_name: power_of_two to 
    # mask dict.
    for val in max_values:
        log2_val = np.ceil(np.log2(val))
        # Pandas dataframes only support a minimum float size of 16 bits, so if the next highest power of two is 8, typing a column as float8 will 
        # cause an error.
        if str(dataset[num_cols[ind]].dtype)[0] == 'f' and log2_val < 16:
            log2_val = 16
        # Integers have a smallest size of 8.
        if str(dataset[num_cols[ind]].dtype)[0] == 'i' and log2_val < 8:
            log2_val = 8
        max_mask[num_cols[ind]] = str(dataset[num_cols[ind]].dtype)[:-2] + str(int(2**np.ceil(np.log2(log2_val))))
        ind += 1
        
    # print(max_mask)
    return max_mask
In [193]:
# Use the function to create mask for resizing the dataframe for improved memory performance.
typing_mask = lowest_num(df)

I originally created the function to do the same thing to both the original dataframe and the no zeros dataframe, only to realize that both dataframes would have the same mask, so calling the function on both dataframes wouldn't actually make any sense. Oh well.

Next is to check for duplicate rows.

Another way to reduce memory usage is by changing low-cardinality fields to a category data type. Changing the 'genre' field may work.

Data Cleaning¶

Tip: Make sure that you keep your reader informed on the steps that you are taking in your investigation. Follow every code cell, or every set of related code cells, with a markdown cell to describe to the reader what was found in the preceding cell(s). Try to make it so that the reader can then understand what they will be seeing in the following cell(s).

In [197]:
# Removing duplicates
df.drop_duplicates(inplace=True)
sum(df.duplicated())
Out[197]:
0
In [198]:
# Ran before and after next two cells to see difference
df.dtypes
Out[198]:

imdb_id                  object
popularity              float64

cast                     object

genres                   object
production_companies     object
release_date             object
vote_count                int64
vote_average            float64
release_year              int64
budget_adj              float64
revenue_adj             float64
dtype: object
In [199]:
# Use the typing_mask variable to change numeric datatypes to smaller numeric types.
df = df.astype(typing_mask)
In [200]:
# Confirm change and check dataframe size.
df.info()

The change seems to have worked, and it reduced the size from 1.7+ MB to 1.3+ MB. Next, I want to check how changing the 'genres' field affects the dataset.

In [201]:
# Change genre column to category type.
df = df.astype({'genres': 'category'})
In [202]:
df.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>

memory usage: 1.3+ MB

This doesn't seem to have changed the size much. Let's get a more in depth view of which data type is best for 'genres'.

In [203]:
df['genres'].memory_usage()
Out[203]:
191050
In [204]:
df = df.astype({'genres': 'string'})
df['genres'].memory_usage()
Out[204]:
173840

Keeping 'genres' as string seems to use less memory, so that's how I'll keep it.
Looking back on an earlier cell where df.head() was called, the reason there are so many different values in the genre column is probably because each film has a set of multiple genres, and there are a little over 2000 unique sets of genres, not 2000 genres.

Next, I want to change the 'release_date' field to a datetime format, rather than string.

In [205]:
# Changin release_date to a datetime object
df['release_date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['release_date'], format='%m/%d/%y')
df.info()
print(type(df['release_date'][0]))

Taylor and two other astronauts come out of de... 112 Adventure|Science Fiction|Mystery Twentieth Century Fox Film Corporation|APJAC P... 2068-02-07 469 7.199219 1968 3.635999e+07 209354704.0

A group of people try to survive an attack of ... 96 Horror Laurel Group|Off Color Films|Image Ten|Market ... 2068-10-01 279 7.199219 1968 7.146619e+05 0.0

Boston is being terrorized by a series of seem... 116 Crime|Drama|Mystery|Thriller Twentieth Century Fox Film Corporation 2068-10-08 15 6.601562 1968 0.000000e+00 0.0

Grand Prix driver Pete Aron is fired by his te... 176 Action|Adventure|Drama Cherokee Productions|Joel Productions|Douglas ... 2066-12-21 20 5.699219 1966 0.000000e+00 0.0
10863 39768 tt0060161 0.065125 0 0 Beregis Avtomobilya Innokentiy Smoktunovskiy|Oleg Efremov|Georgi Z... NaN Eldar Ryazanov NaN ... An insurance agent who moonlights as a carthie... 94 Mystery|Comedy Mosfilm 2066-01-01 11 6.500000 1966 0.000000e+00 0.0
10864 21449 tt0061177 0.064331 0 0 What's Up, Tiger Lily? Tatsuya Mihashi|Akiko Wakabayashi|Mie Hama|Joh... NaN Woody Allen WOODY ALLEN STRIKES BACK! ... In comic Woody Allen's film debut, he took the... 80 Action|Comedy Benedict Pictures Corp. 2066-11-02 22 5.398438 1966 0.000000e+00 0.0
10865 22293 tt0060666 0.035919 19000 0 Manos: The Hands of Fate Harold P. Warren|Tom Neyman|John Reynolds|Dian... NaN Harold P. Warren It's Shocking! It's Beyond Your Imagination! ... A family gets lost on the road and stumbles up... 74 Horror Norm-Iris 2066-11-15 15 1.500000 1966 1.276423e+05 0.0

331 rows × 21 columns

The original dataframe contains the dates in the format of mm/dd/yy. That is, the year in each date is only represented by two numbers. The way .to_datetime() handles two-digit years is by selecting a two-digit number, in this case 69, and setting any two-digit year lower than it as being in the 21st century. This works great for numbers like 13, which get converted to 2013, but in the dataset, there are numbers above 60, but below 69, so using the method creates entries with a release date in the future. This needs to be dealt with.

In [209]:
predate_mask = df['release_date'] > pd.to_datetime('2030', format='%Y')
df.loc[predate_mask, 'release_date'] -= pd.DateOffset(years=100)
# This seems to cause issues with the data format, so I will need to reformat the 'release_date' field
df['release_date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['release_date'], format='%m/%d/%y')
In [211]:
# Check to make sure no more entries are from the future
df[df['release_date'] > pd.to_datetime(2030, format='%Y')]
Out[211]:
id imdb_id popularity budget revenue original_title cast homepage director tagline ... overview runtime genres production_companies release_date vote_count vote_average release_year budget_adj revenue_adj

0 rows × 21 columns

Next, any fields I don't plan on using during analysis should be removed. This will reduce dataset size and also help to get rid of many of the null values, so when I remove rows with nulls, as few rows as possible get removed.

In [212]:
# Dropping unnecessary fields
df.drop(['release_year', 'homepage', 'tagline', 'keywords', 'overview', 'production_companies', 'cast', 'imdb_id'], axis=1, inplace=True)
df.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
Index: 10865 entries, 0 to 10865
Data columns (total 13 columns):
 #   Column          Non-Null Count  Dtype         
---  ------          --------------  -----         
 0   id              10865 non-null  int32         
 1   popularity      10865 non-null  float16       
 2   budget          10865 non-null  int32         
 3   revenue         10865 non-null  int32         
 4   original_title  10865 non-null  object        
 5   director        10821 non-null  object        
 6   runtime         10865 non-null  int16         
 7   genres          10842 non-null  string        
 8   release_date    10865 non-null  datetime64[ns]
 9   vote_count      10865 non-null  int16         
 10  vote_average    10865 non-null  float16       
 11  budget_adj      10865 non-null  float32       
 12  revenue_adj     10865 non-null  float32       
dtypes: datetime64[ns](1), float16(2), float32(2), int16(2), int32(3), object(2), string(1)
memory usage: 979.5+ KB
In [213]:
# Remove null values. 
df = df.dropna(how='any', axis=0)
df.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
Index: 10800 entries, 0 to 10865
Data columns (total 13 columns):
 #   Column          Non-Null Count  Dtype

In [214]:
df.head(1)
Out[214]:
id popularity budget revenue original_title director runtime genres release_date vote_count vote_average budget_adj revenue_adj
0 135397 33.0 150000000 1513528810 Jurassic World Colin Trevorrow 124 Action|Adventure|Science Fiction|Thriller 2015-06-09 5562 6.5 137999936.0 1.392446e+09
In [215]:
# Create scatter matrix to check for possible correlations. This may return a RuntimeWarning. The code will still run, though.
axes = pd.plotting.scatter_matrix(df, alpha=0.2, figsize=(12, 10))
for ax in axes.flatten():
    ax.xaxis.label.set_rotation(90)
    ax.yaxis.label.set_rotation(0)
    ax.yaxis.label.set_ha('right')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.gcf().subplots_adjust(wspace=0, hspace=0)
plt.show()

C:\Users\joshu\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python39\lib\site-packages\pandas\plotting\_matplotlib\misc.py:69: RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in scalar subtract
  rdelta_ext = (rmax_ - rmin_) * range_padding / 2

Weirdly, revenue graphs seem to start in the middle. That RuntimeWarning is also concerning. I wonder what's going on.

In [216]:
# Check for revenue values less than 0
df.query('revenue < 0')
Out[216]:
id popularity budget revenue original_title director runtime genres release_date vote_count vote_average budget_adj revenue_adj
1386 19995 9.429688 237000000 -1513461449 Avatar James Cameron 162 Action|Adventure|Fantasy|Science Fiction 2009-12-10 8458 7.101562 240886896.0 2.827124e+09

It seems that this is the cause of the problem. James Cameron's "Avatar" seems to have a negative revenue. This also doesn't seem to be a separate "Avatar" entry with the wrong value in place, as seen below.

In [217]:
df.query('original_title == "Avatar"')
Out[217]:
id popularity budget revenue original_title director runtime genres release_date vote_count vote_average budget_adj revenue_adj
1386 19995 9.429688 237000000 -1513461449 Avatar James Cameron 162 Action|Adventure|Fantasy|Science Fiction 2009-12-10 8458 7.101562 240886896.0 2.827124e+09

Let's check how each films 'revenue_adj' field compares to the 'revenue' field.

In [218]:
print((df[df['revenue'] > 0]['revenue_adj'] - df[df['revenue'] > 0]['revenue']).mean())

25863259.707041763

The average revenue for each film seems to be around 26,000,000 below the adjusted revenue. Let's use that value to update Avatar's revenue.

In [219]:
df.loc[df['original_title'] == 'Avatar', 'revenue'] = df.query('original_title == "Avatar"')['revenue_adj'] + 25863259
In [220]:
# Rerun scatter_matrix code. No longer shows RuntimeWarning.
axes = pd.plotting.scatter_matrix(df, alpha=0.2, figsize=(12, 10))
for ax in axes.flatten():
    ax.xaxis.label.set_rotation(90)
    ax.yaxis.label.set_rotation(0)
    ax.yaxis.label.set_ha('right')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.gcf().subplots_adjust(wspace=0, hspace=0)
plt.show()

Now that there aren't any revenue values below 0, we no longer get a RuntimeWarning, and the graphs for revenue no longer start in the middle. However, there are still many entries with 0 revenue or budget, so no is the time to create a new dataset without those values.

In [221]:
# Creating separate dataframe without the 0 values
df_no_zero = df[df['revenue'] != 0]
df_no_zero = df_no_zero[df_no_zero['budget'] != 0]
df_no_zero.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
Index: 3853 entries, 0 to 10848
Data columns (total 13 columns):
 #   Column          Non-Null Count  Dtype         
---  ------          --------------  -----         
 0   id              3853 non-null   int32         
 1   popularity      3853 non-null   float16       
 2   budget          3853 non-null   int32         
 3   revenue         3853 non-null   float64       
 4   original_title  3853 non-null   object        
 5   director        3853 non-null   object        
 6   runtime         3853 non-null   int16         
 7   genres          3853 non-null   string        
 8   release_date    3853 non-null   datetime64[ns]
 9   vote_count      3853 non-null   int16         
 10  vote_average    3853 non-null   float16       
 11  budget_adj      3853 non-null   float32       
 12  revenue_adj     3853 non-null   float32       
dtypes: datetime64[ns](1), float16(2), float32(2), float64(1), int16(2), int32(2), object(2), string(1)
memory usage: 270.9+ KB
In [222]:
axes = pd.plotting.scatter_matrix(df_no_zero, alpha=0.2, figsize=(12, 10))
for ax in axes.flatten():
    ax.xaxis.label.set_rotation(90)
    ax.yaxis.label.set_rotation(0)
    ax.yaxis.label.set_ha('right')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.gcf().subplots_adjust(wspace=0, hspace=0)
plt.show()

Some data seems to have shifted, but most of it seems fairly similar. However, the size of the no_zero dataset is significantly less, only containing a little under 4000 rows.

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Tip: Now that you've trimmed and cleaned your data, you're ready to move on to exploration. Compute statistics and create visualizations with the goal of addressing the research questions that you posed in the Introduction section. You should compute the relevant statistics throughout the analysis when an inference is made about the data. Note that at least two or more kinds of plots should be created as part of the exploration, and you must compare and show trends in the varied visualizations. Remember to utilize the visualizations that the pandas library already has available.

Tip: Investigate the stated question(s) from multiple angles. It is recommended that you be systematic with your approach. Look at one variable at a time, and then follow it up by looking at relationships between variables. You should explore at least three variables in relation to the primary question. This can be an exploratory relationship between three variables of interest, or looking at how two independent variables relate to a single dependent variable of interest. Lastly, you should perform both single-variable (1d) and multiple-variable (2d) explorations.

How have revenue and budget changed over the years?¶

In [225]:
# Use this, and more code cells, to explore your data. Don't forget to add
#   Markdown cells to document your observations and findings.

# Find the time window for analysis
print(df['release_date'].min(), df['release_date'].max())
df.info()

1960-01-01 00:00:00 2015-12-31 00:00:00
<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
Index: 10800 entries, 0 to 10865
Data columns (total 13 columns):
 #   Column          Non-Null Count  Dtype         
---  ------          --------------  -----         
 0   id              10800 non-null  int32

dtypes: datetime64[ns](1), float16(2), float32(3), int16(2), int32(2), object(2), string(1)
memory usage: 717.2+ KB

Revenue seems to have been reverted to a float64 after that last calculation, so I need to change it back.

In [236]:
typing_mask = lowest_num(df)
df = df.astype(typing_mask)
df_no_zero = df[df['revenue'] != 0]
df_no_zero = df_no_zero[df_no_zero['budget'] != 0]
In [237]:
df['release_date'].dt.year.value_counts().sort_index().plot(kind='bar', figsize=(12, 6))
Out[237]:
<Axes: xlabel='release_date'>

The number of films per year seems to increase in the dataset. However, it is entirely possible that this is due to the dataset being incomplete, and only having significant data for more recent years.

In [238]:
df.plot(x='revenue', y='budget', kind='scatter')
Out[238]:
<Axes: xlabel='revenue', ylabel='budget'>

There appears to be a positive correlation between revenue and budget. However, this correlation seems fairly weak.

In [239]:
# Define functions for more easily creating various graphs for release_date, budget, and revenue from defined dataframe. Could be modified to 
# accept any graph by creating x and y arguments and adding more graph types to the function. Second and third function work together to 
# create two seperate graphs for both revenue and budget.

def quick_graph(data, graph, sep=False, x='release_date', y=['revenue', 'budget']):
    if not sep:
        ax = data.plot(alpha=0.5, x=x, y=y[0], kind=graph, color='Blue', label=y[0], figsize=(10, 4))
        if len(y) > 1:
            data.plot(alpha=0.5, x=x, y=y[1], kind=graph, color='Red', label=y[1], ax=ax, figsize=(10, 4))
            ax.legend(loc='upper right')
    else:
        data.plot(alpha=0.5, x=x, y=y[0], kind=graph, color='Blue', label=y[0], figsize=(10, 4))
        if len(y) > 1:
            data.plot(alpha=0.5, x=x, y=y[1], kind=graph, color='Red', label=y[1], figsize=(10, 4))
            plt.show()


def tend_graph(data, tend, fieldname, color='Blue', group='release_date'):
    if group == 'release_date':
        if tend == 'mean':
            data.groupby(pd.Grouper(key=group, freq='YE'))[fieldname].mean().plot(kind='line', color=color, label=fieldname, figsize=(10, 4), legend=True)
        if tend == 'median':
            data.groupby(pd.Grouper(key=group, freq='YE'))[fieldname].median().plot(kind='line', color=color, label=fieldname, figsize=(10, 4), legend=True)
    else:
        if tend == 'mean':
            data.groupby(by=group)[fieldname].mean().plot(kind='line', color=color, label=fieldname, figsize=(10, 4), legend=True)
        if tend == 'median':
            data.groupby(by=group)[fieldname].median().plot(kind='line', color=color, label=fieldname, figsize=(10, 4), legend=True)


def quick_line(data, tend, fields=['revenue', 'budget']):
    print(tend_graph(data, tend, fields[0], color='Blue'))
    print(tend_graph(data, tend, fields[1], color='Red'))
In [240]:
quick_graph(df, 'scatter')

As seen by these two graphs, with or without the zero values, budget and revenue appear to be increasing over time. Or at least, the maximum of both seems to increase. The minimum seems to largely stay the same.

In [241]:
quick_graph(df_no_zero, graph='scatter')

Here are the two graphs separated for the non-zero set.

In [242]:
print(quick_graph(df_no_zero, graph='scatter', sep=True))

None

While separate, it appears that budget is increasing at a faster rate. However, the maximum value listed for budget is 1e8, while revenue is 1e9. That is an order of magnitude different, and looking at the graphs together rather than separate as done in the first two graphs shows this.

Both revenue and budget have been increasing at a steady rate, and budget rate of increase seems to be higher than revenue. However, revenue still seems to be above budget, but that only appears to be the case if you look at the maximum values in the scatter graph, shown by the tops of the dense clusters. Looking at the mean, however, shows that they appear to have a tighter correlation, with both steadily increasing until around the year 2000, then seemingly starting to decrease.

In [243]:
quick_line(df, 'mean')

None
None

This trend is only when zeros are in the dataset, and removing them shows that revenue continues to increase, and budget seems to flatten off.

In [244]:
quick_line(df_no_zero, 'mean')

None
None

Using the median values shows a similar trend, though the graph with zeros doesn't really show any trend at all.

In [245]:
quick_line(df, 'median')

None
None
In [246]:
quick_line(df_no_zero, 'median')

None
None

There appears to be an outlier early on in the median graph. What could that be about?

In [247]:
print(df_no_zero.groupby(pd.Grouper(key='release_date', freq='YE'))['revenue'].median())

Freq: YE-DEC, Name: revenue, dtype: float64
In [249]:
df_no_zero[df_no_zero['revenue'] == 111721912]
Out[249]:
id popularity budget revenue original_title director runtime genres release_date vote_count vote_average budget_adj revenue_adj
10691 907 1.282227 14000000 111721912.0 Doctor Zhivago David Lean 197 Drama|Romance|War 1965-12-22 126 6.898438 96887888.0 773178624.0

I have never heard of this film, but I guess 1965 was a good year for movies. Actually, only 5 movies from that year are in the dataset, so using central tendency values for this year may produce outliers when mixed with other years that have many more entries. By grouping by year, we can see a count of how many films the dataset has for each year, and we can see that later years have more films per year. This does not necessarily mean that more films get released each year, though that is a possibility, but it could also mean that this dataset is incomplete, and only has extensive data for more recent years. This trend is also seen in the histogram above.

In [250]:
print(df_no_zero.groupby(pd.Grouper(key='release_date', freq='YE'))['revenue'].count())
print()

df_no_zero[df_no_zero['release_date'].dt.year == 1965]
Out[251]:
id popularity budget revenue original_title director runtime genres release_date vote_count vote_average budget_adj revenue_adj
10689 660 1.910156 11000000 141195664.0 Thunderball Terence Young 130 Adventure|Action|Thriller 1965-12-16 331 6.300781 76126200.0 9.771535e+08

10691 907 1.282227 14000000 111721912.0 Doctor Zhivago David Lean 197 Drama|Romance|War 1965-12-22 126 6.898438 96887888.0 7.731786e+08

In [252]:
df_no_zero[df_no_zero['release_date'].dt.year == 1965].describe()
Out[252]:
id popularity budget revenue runtime release_date vote_count vote_average budget_adj revenue_adj

In any case, it seems that revenue and budget are correlated, and are increasing every year, though budget increases may be slowing down.

Just for fun, let's see how profit has changed over the years as well.

In [253]:
df['profit'] = df['revenue'] - df['budget']
df_no_zero['profit'] = df_no_zero['revenue'] - df_no_zero['budget']
In [254]:
print(quick_graph(df, graph='scatter', y=['profit']))
print(quick_graph(df_no_zero, graph='scatter', y=['profit']))

None
None
In [255]:
tend_graph(df, 'mean', 'profit', 'Blue')
tend_graph(df_no_zero, 'mean', 'profit', 'Red')
print('Red line is mean profits without zeros. \nBlue line is with.')
plt.show()
print('Orange line is median profits without zeros. \nGreen line is with.')
tend_graph(df, 'median', 'profit', 'Green')
tend_graph(df_no_zero, 'median', 'profit', 'Orange')

Red line is mean profits without zeros. 
Blue line is with.

Orange line is median profits without zeros. 
Green line is with.

Note that profit can still be zero in the no_zero dataframe. Only films with a revenue or budget of zero have been removed, which is why the green line mostly shows zero. Refer to the median graph that included zeros in the dataset, and see that it mostly is flat with a few spikes for both profit and revenue.

Question 1 conclusion:¶

Both budget and revenue have increased over time, though since revenue seems to have been increasing at a faster rate, total profits have also been slightly increasing over time, as seen in the final graph. However, while mean profits seem to increase significantly, median profits largely seem to stay the same. Outliers and large spikes early on in the graphs are likely from years with few films, all or most of which did fairly well. Using central tendency with few data points tends to make it harder to show accurate trends over time, but a general trend can still be seen.

How does genre affect popularity?¶

To start, we should probably scale down the dataset, since there is only one 'genres' column, but mutiple genres per film. As such, I am going to split the 'genres' column to account for all of the genres. The fact that each cell in the column contains a set of various different genres is probably why there are so many unique values in the column.

In [256]:
# Let's start by showing the columns to decide which to get rid of in the new dataset.
df.head(1)
Out[256]:
id popularity budget revenue original_title director runtime genres release_date vote_count vote_average budget_adj revenue_adj profit
0 135397 33.0 150000000 1.513529e+09 Jurassic World Colin Trevorrow 124 Action|Adventure|Science Fiction|Thriller 2015-06-09 5562 6.5 137999936.0 1.392446e+09 1.363529e+09
In [257]:
# We don't need most of these. 'id' will be kept, as having a primary key is almost always a good idea for a table, but most of these can go.
drop_columns = ['budget', 'runtime', 'vote_count', 'vote_average', 'budget_adj', 'revenue_adj', 'profit']
df_genres = df.drop(drop_columns, axis=1)
In [269]:
df_genres = df.drop(drop_columns, axis=1)
In [270]:
df_genres.head(1)
Out[270]:
id popularity revenue original_title director genres release_date
0 135397 33.0 1.513529e+09 Jurassic World Colin Trevorrow Action|Adventure|Science Fiction|Thriller 2015-06-09
In [271]:
# Split each string value in the column to a list of strings
df_genres['genres'] = df_genres['genres'].str.split(pat='|').to_list()
In [272]:
df_genres.head(1)
Out[272]:
id popularity revenue original_title director genres release_date
0 135397 33.0 1.513529e+09 Jurassic World Colin Trevorrow [Action, Adventure, Science Fiction, Thriller] 2015-06-09
In [273]:
# Split each list in the column to create multiple rows for each entry, each with a single genre
df_genres = df_genres.explode(column='genres')
In [274]:
df_genres.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
Index: 26864 entries, 0 to 10865
Data columns (total 7 columns):
 #   Column          Non-Null Count  Dtype         
---  ------          --------------  -----         
 0   id              26864 non-null  int32         
 1   popularity      26864 non-null  float16       
 2   revenue         26864 non-null  float32       
 3   original_title  26864 non-null  object        
 4   director        26864 non-null  object        
 5   genres          26864 non-null  object        
 6   release_date    26864 non-null  datetime64[ns]
dtypes: datetime64[ns](1), float16(1), float32(1), int32(1), object(3)
memory usage: 1.3+ MB
In [275]:
df_genres['genres'].nunique()
Out[275]:
20

Now that there are only 20 unique genre values, changing it to type category should decrease the size.

In [276]:
df_genres['genres'].memory_usage()
Out[276]:
429824
In [277]:
df_genres = df_genres.astype({'genres': 'category'})
In [278]:
df_genres['genres'].memory_usage()
Out[278]:
242492

With the new low cardinality, changing 'genres' to category nearly halved the field's size.

In [279]:
print(df_genres['genres'].nunique())
print(df_genres['genres'].value_counts())

20
genres
Drama              4754
Comedy             3782
Thriller           2904
Action             2378
Romance            1708
Horror             1636
Adventure          1466
Crime              1353
Science Fiction    1223
Family             1223
Fantasy             912
Mystery             809
Animation           692
Documentary         509
Music               402
History             332
War                 270
Foreign             185
Western             164
TV Movie            162
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [280]:
# This is the closest I could get the histogram's bars to line up with the labels. Having bins='stone' or 'sqrt' will also work, but the bars become 
# much smaller.
df_genres['genres'].hist(figsize=(10, 6), xrot=90, align='mid', bins='rice', grid=False)
Out[280]:
<Axes: >
In [281]:
df_genres.groupby(by='genres', observed=False)['popularity'].median().sort_values(ascending=False).plot(kind='bar', figsize=(10, 4), title='Median Popularity per Genre')
plt.show()
df_genres.groupby(by='genres', observed=False)['popularity'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False).plot(kind='bar', figsize=(10, 4), title='Mean Popularity per Genre');

The order changes slightly depending on whether mean or median is used. To clarify, median can help to remove the influence of outliers in datasets. There is a way to check for outliers.

In [282]:
Q1 = df_genres['popularity'].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = df_genres['popularity'].quantile(0.75)
iqr = Q3-Q1

df_genre_outliers = df_genres[(df_genres['popularity'] < (Q1 - 1.5 * iqr)) | (df_genres['popularity'] > (Q3 + 1.5 * iqr))]
In [283]:
df_genre_outliers['genres'].value_counts()
Out[283]:
genres
Action             333
Adventure          293
Drama              276
Comedy             250
Thriller           250
Science Fiction    184
Fantasy            159
Family             151
Romance            119
Crime              117
Animation          108
Mystery             62
Horror              51
War                 30
Music               22
History             19
Western              7
TV Movie             1
Foreign              0
Documentary          0
Name: count, dtype: int64

It seems that various genres have some outliers. However, the genres with the most outliers also are also genres with a lot of entries in the dataset. In order to see how much the outliers affect the mean of each genre, more work needs to be done.

In [284]:
df_out_neg = df_genres[(df_genres['popularity'] < (Q1 - 1.5 * iqr))]
df_out_pos = df_genres[(df_genres['popularity'] > (Q3 + 1.5 * iqr))]
print(100 * (df_out_pos['genres'].value_counts()) / (df_genres['genres'].value_counts()))
print(100 * (df_out_neg['genres'].value_counts()) / (df_genres['genres'].value_counts()))

genres
Action             14.003364
Adventure          19.986357
Animation          15.606936
Comedy              6.610259
Crime               8.647450
Documentary         0.000000
Drama               5.805637
Family             12.346688
Fantasy            17.434211
Foreign             0.000000
History             5.722892
Horror              3.117359
Music               5.472637
Mystery             7.663782
Romance             6.967213
Science Fiction    15.044971
TV Movie            0.617284
Thriller            8.608815
War                11.111111
Western             4.268293
Name: count, dtype: float64
genres
Action             0.0
Adventure          0.0
Animation          0.0
Comedy             0.0
Crime              0.0
Documentary        0.0
Drama              0.0
Family             0.0
Fantasy            0.0
Foreign            0.0
History            0.0
Horror             0.0
Music              0.0
Mystery            0.0
Romance            0.0
Science Fiction    0.0
TV Movie           0.0
Thriller           0.0
War                0.0
Western            0.0
Name: count, dtype: float64

Seemingly, there aren't any outliers bringing down the mean, but some genres are nearly 20% outliers. However, the actual number of outliers seems to affect the mean more than the percent of data that is outliers. Looking at the percentages and the above graphs does not seem to explain the difference between mean and median, as the genres with high percentages don't necessarily lower in popularity from mean to median, with some of them even increasing in rank. When looking at the flat number of outliers, genres with more are more likely to have a higher mean compared to the median.

In [285]:
df_genre_no_zero = df_genres[df_genres['revenue'] != 0]
In [286]:
df_genres.groupby(by='genres', observed=False)['popularity'].median().sort_values(ascending=False).plot(kind='bar', figsize=(10, 4), title='Median Popularity With Zeros')
plt.show()
df_genre_no_zero.groupby(by='genres', observed=False)['popularity'].median().sort_values(ascending=False).plot(kind='bar', figsize=(10, 4), title='Median Popularity Without Zeros');

Finally, removing the entries with 0 revenue changes the rankings somewhat significantly. The 'Crime' genre moves down 4 spaces, and 'Science Fiction' moves down 3. 'Animation' and 'Adventure' swap places as well.

Question 2 Conclusion¶

Animation, Fantasy, and Adventure seem to be the most popular genres, while Foreign, Documentary, and TV Movies seem to be the least popular.

Conclusions¶

Question 1: How have revenue and budget changed over the years?¶

Both budget and revenue, between the years 1960 and 2015, have largely increased, with revenue incresing at a faster rate, so overall mean profits have increased over time. However, median profits have not changed much. Importantly, it is highly likely that the dataset is incomplete, as I stated during the exploration portion, leading to more jagged graphs that may be harder to discern accurate trends.

Question 2: How does genre affect popularity?¶

Adventure, Animation, and Fantasy films tend to be the most popular, while TV Movies, Documentary, and Foreign films tend to be the least popular.
However, as stated before, the dataset seems to be incomplete, making accurate analysis difficult. Also, most genres don't really have a rigid definition, so while two films may be labeled as "Fantasy", the actual content of each may be vastly different. For example, "Star Wars" and "The Lord of the Rings" are both "fantasy", but one is high fantasy, while the other is sci-fi. Due to the nebulous definition of each genre, while one can see how popular each genre is, it is far more difficult to determin how popular a film will be just from the listed genres.

Submitting your Project¶

Tip: Before you submit your project, you need to create a .html or .pdf version of this notebook in the workspace here. To do that, run the code cell below. If it worked correctly, you should see output that starts with NbConvertApp] Converting notebook, and you should see the generated .html file in the workspace directory (click on the orange Jupyter icon in the upper left).

Tip: Alternatively, you can download this report as .html via the File > Download as submenu, and then manually upload it into the workspace directory by clicking on the orange Jupyter icon in the upper left, then using the Upload button.

Tip: Once you've done this, you can submit your project by clicking on the "Submit Project" button in the lower right here. This will create and submit a zip file with this .ipynb doc and the .html or .pdf version you created. Congratulations!
